subject: Mozi Minute: The Angry Boat
body:
When I was 18, I worked for a fur coat dealer.

One day, a lady storms in, furious about a missing button on her expensive coat.

The owner rolled his eyes towards me in the back of the store, out of eyesight.

He got up, then transformed his face as he rounded the corner to the retail area.

He took it up a notch "THAT'S RIDICULOUS! Who sold you this coat?"

He grabbed it from her to inspect the grace indiscretion.

"Give me their name. I'll get to the bottom of this right away. They're going to be held accountable for this! Did anyone see you in it? This is completely unacceptable."

Suddenly, she's backpedaling:

"Well, it's not THAT bad..."

He fired back.

"It is that bad. We'll get this taken care of right away."

He handed it to the seamstress in the back.

She sewed the button on in minutes.

He chit chatted and handed her back her coat within a few minutes.

"Of course, this is on the house. My deepest apologies. Ridiculous."

"Oh you're the best thank you for always looking out for us."

She'd completely changed her tune.

She left. The door closed. As soon as she left he grinned at me.

"There can only be one person in the angry boat."

This taught me the most powerful customer service lesson of my life.

How to deal with angry customers:

Step 1: Match Their Energy (Plus 20%)
Step 2: Direct Anger at the Problem (Not Them)
Step 3: Take Immediate Dramatic Action
Step 4: Watch Them Calm Down

They came ready to fight. But you're on their side, fighting harder than they are.

There's no one left to fight.

I've trained every support team I've ever had on the angry boat since.

It works every time.

The customer who was about to leave a one-star review becomes your biggest advocate.

Try it next time and let me know how it goes,

- Alex
